title: Builder Pattern: The Complete Walkthrough
subtitle: From telescoping constructors to fluent validated builders -- an interactive discussion that builds the pattern step by step, through questions, doubts, and aha moments.

content.value: Imagine a restaurant ordering system. Every meal is customizable: bun type, patty count, cheese, veggies (lettuce, tomato, onion, jalapeno), sauces (ketchup, mayo, BBQ), extras (bacon, egg, avocado), and meal size. You write a constructor:

content.title: The Problem: Telescoping Constructor

content.value: Tomorrow the restaurant adds 'spice level'. Your constructor now has 16 parameters. Every. Single. Existing. Call. Breaks. A developer mixes up parameter order -- true goes into the cheese slot, 'lettuce' goes into the sauce slot. It compiles. It runs. It's WRONG.
The real problem isn't just 'too many parameters.' It's that parameter ORDER is the only contract, and booleans are indistinguishable. new Meal(..., true, false, true, false) is a time bomb.

content.question: Telescoping constructors are ugly. Why not use a no-arg constructor and setters? meal.setBun('sesame'); meal.setCheese('cheddar'); -- clean and readable.
content.answer:
Setters solve readability but create a WORSE problem: transient invalid states. Between new Meal() and the last setter, the object is half-built. Is it a valid meal after setBun() but before setSize()? Nobody knows. There's no 'done' signal.

Worse: setters mean the object is PERMANENTLY mutable. Someone can call meal.setCheese('swiss') AFTER the meal was sent to the kitchen. And in multi-threaded systems, Thread A calls setters while Thread B reads -- you get a half-baked burger.

content.title: Setter Problems

content.value: Setters give you readability but take away safety. You need BOTH: step-by-step configuration (like setters) AND atomic validated creation (like constructors).

sections.title: Builder: The Best of Both Worlds

content.value: What if the thing you call setters on ISN'T the actual object? What if it's a separate staging area whose only job is to collect configuration and then produce the final, perfect, validated object in ONE shot?

content.title: Builder Pattern Solution
content.value:
// Clean, readable, self-documenting
Meal burger = new Meal.Builder()
    .bun("sesame")
    .pattyCount(2)
    .cheese("cheddar")
    .lettuce(true)
    .bacon(true)
    .sauce("BBQ")
    .size("large")
    .build();  // validates, computes price, returns IMMUTABLE Meal

// Simple order -- optional stuff just isn't called
Meal simple = new Meal.Builder()
    .bun("regular")
    .pattyCount(1)
    .size("small")
    .build();

// Cross-field validation catches errors
Meal invalid = new Meal.Builder()
    .bun("gluten-free")
    .bacon(true)
    .build();  // THROWS: "Bacon not available with gluten-free bun"

The Builder collects choices. The Meal is born only at build() -- fully validated, all computed fields calculated, ready to use. You can change your mind on the Builder (it's a draft). Once build() is called, you know the object was born valid.
Builder = staging area. Product = validated artifact. build() = the gate between draft and done. Configure freely before build(). The object is guaranteed valid at birth.
Every setter in the Builder returns 'this' -- the Builder itself. This one decision enables the entire fluent API.

content.title: return this = fluent chaining

content.value: 'return this' turns a sequence of commands into a readable sentence. It's one line that makes the entire pattern ergonomic.

sections.title: build() Is Where the Real Power Lives

content.value: Most tutorials show build() as just 'return new Product(this)'. That's missing the POINT. build() is the gatekeeper -- it validates, computes, and guarantees correctness.

content.title: build() with real validation

content.question: Why not validate in each setter instead of in build()?
content.answer:
Because some validations span MULTIPLE fields. You can't check 'gluten-free + bacon' in setBun() because bacon hasn't been set yet. And you can't check it in setBacon() because bun might change later. Only build() has the COMPLETE picture.

Also, the user might set bun to gluten-free, then set bacon to true, then change bun to regular -- that's VALID. If you validated in setters, the intermediate state would throw.

content.value: Validate in setters = premature. Validate in build() = complete picture. build() sees ALL fields and can check ANY cross-field constraint.

sections.title: Immutability: A Choice, Not a Requirement

content.value: Many tutorials say Builder MUST produce immutable objects. That's not true. Builder's job is clean construction + validation. Immutability is a separate design decision that pairs well with Builder but isn't mandatory.

content.title: Value Objects → Immutable (all final, no setters)
Entities → Identity final, Lifecycle mutable

content.value: Builder guarantees the object is BORN VALID. Whether it stays frozen (immutable value object) or lives and changes (mutable entity) is a separate design decision. Don't force immutability where it doesn't belong.
When the same Builder chain appears in 2+ places, extract it into a Director. The Director knows the RECIPE. The Builder knows the STEPS.

content.title: Director pattern

content.value: Director = DRY for Builder. If a recipe appears twice, extract it. Adding a new recipe doesn't change Builder or Product.
Most tutorials stop at 'Builder is for lots of parameters.' That's surface-level. Builder is for any situation where the CONSTRUCTION PROCESS has rules, steps, and validation.

content.title: Builder in unexpected places
content.value:
// SQL Query Builder -- grammar rules, not just params
String sql = new QueryBuilder()
    .select("name", "email")
    .from("users")
    .where("age > 18")
    .orderBy("name")
    .limit(10)
    .build();

// HTTP Request Builder -- conditional parts
HttpRequest req = HttpRequest.newBuilder()
    .uri(URI.create("/api/users"))
    .header("Authorization", "Bearer " + token)
    .header("Content-Type", "application/json")
    .POST(BodyPublishers.ofString(json))
    .timeout(Duration.ofSeconds(30))
    .build();

// Test Data Builder -- readable test setup
User testUser = new UserBuilder()
    .withName("Alice")
    .withEmail("alice@test.com")
    .withRole(Role.ADMIN)
    .isActive(true)
    .build();
Builder isn't about parameter count. It's about PROCESS. Anywhere construction has rules, ordering, or validation -- Builder belongs.

content.question: Factory and Builder both create objects. When do I use which? And can I combine them?
content.answer:
Factory = WHICH type to create (one decision, one call).
Builder = HOW to configure one type (many steps, fluent API).

Factory: NotificationFactory.create('email') -- picks the type.
Builder: Notification.builder().title('Hi').body('...').build() -- configures the chosen type.

Combine when you need BOTH: Factory picks which Builder to use, Builder does the assembly.

content.title: Factory + Builder together

content.value: Factory = WHAT. Builder = HOW. Together = powerful. Don't confuse them, but don't be afraid to combine them.

sections.title: The Decision Tree: Do I Need Builder?

content.value: After this entire walkthrough, here's your decision checklist:

content.title: Decision Flowchart
content.value:
How many constructor parameters?
  |
  +-- 2-3, all required, no validation? --> Skip Builder. Plain constructor.
  |
  +-- 4+ parameters OR optional fields?
       |
       +-- Cross-field validation needed?
       |    |
       |    +-- YES --> Builder with validation in build()
       |
       +-- Object must be immutable after creation?
       |    |
       |    +-- YES --> Builder with final fields + private constructor
       |
       +-- Computed/derived fields?
       |    |
       |    +-- YES --> Builder computes them in build()
       |
       +-- Same build process, different representations?
       |    |
       |    +-- YES --> Builder + Director
       |
       +-- Kotlin with named args + defaults?
            |
            +-- Simple case --> Named args may suffice
            +-- Complex validation --> Still need Builder
Start simple. Use Builder when construction has RULES. Don't add it 'for the future' -- add it when you FEEL the pain of telescoping constructors or invalid states.
